title: Churn Analysis for Bank Customers
text:
Data Quality Assessment
While checking the statistics of the dataset, I noticed that some numerical features had extreme outliers, so I created boxplots to visualise the distribution of these features and identify the outliers that needed to be handled before proceeding with the analysis.


Code
# columns for boxplots
cols = ['score',
       'age',
       'equity',
       'balance',
       'salary']

# boxplots for numerical features
plt.figure(figsize=(8,7))
for i, col in enumerate(cols):
    sns.boxplot(bank_data[col], color=sns.color_palette('Set3')[i+1], ax=plt.subplot(3, 2, i+1))
    plt.title('Boxplot for {}'.format(col), fontsize=17)
    plt.xlabel(col)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









As expected, the balance sheet and salary data contain many outliers. The “equity” and “age” features also contain outliers, but they are few and small, so we decided to leave them unchanged. To more accurately determine which data to discard, we calculated the 95th, 97.5, and 99th percentiles. After this, we decided to discard balance and salary data at the 95th percentile.

title: Churn Analysis for Bank Customers
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis
The first step was to understand how each feature relates to customer churn. The two charts below highlight credit score and city distributions, which showed interesting initial patterns.


Code
# Dataset preprocessing
bank_data['churn'] = bank_data['churn'].astype('int')
bank_data['gender'] = bank_data['gender'].replace('Ж', 0)
bank_data['gender'] = bank_data['gender'].replace('М', 1)

b_data = bank_data.query('balance < 2300000 or balance.isna()')
b_data = b_data.query('salary < 300000')
bd = pd.get_dummies(b_data)

# churn distribution by score and age
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 3))
sns.histplot(data=b_data, x='score', stat='density', palette='Set2',
             ax=axes[0], hue='churn', multiple='dodge', common_norm=False)
axes[0].set_title('Churn by Credit Score')

sns.histplot(data=b_data, x='age', stat='density', palette='Set2',
             ax=axes[1], hue='churn', multiple='dodge', common_norm=False)
axes[1].set_title('Churn by Age')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Churn distribution by credit score and age










NoteClick to see all feature distributions







Code
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 20))
for i, col in enumerate(bd.drop(['churn', 'userid'], axis=1).columns):
    sns.histplot(data=bd, x=col, stat='density', palette='Set2',
                 ax=plt.subplot(6, 2, i+1), hue='churn',
                 multiple='dodge', common_norm=False)
    plt.title(f'Churn distribution for {col}', fontsize=15)
    plt.xlabel(col)
    plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Churn distribution across all features







Also we created a correlation matrix to understand the relationships between features and identify any multicollinearity issues.


Code
# correlation matrix
corr = bd.corr()
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, fmt='.0%', cmap='vlag');
plt.title('Correlation Matrix', fontsize=17);





Correlation matrix of features




The highest correlation—44%—is between credit rating and the number of properties owned. This indicator likely carries significant weight when calculating the rating. We also see an average correlation (36%) between rating and churn, with a high rating corresponding to high churn.
We also see an average correlation of 33% between balance and salary. Also, having a credit card has an average negative correlation with churn.
After calculating the correlation matrix, we decided to calculate the phik correlation matrix to better understand the relationships between features, especially since some of them are categorical.


Code
plt.figure(figsize=(8,7))
plt.subplot(1, 2, 1)
sns.heatmap(pd.DataFrame(corr['churn']).drop(index='churn').sort_values('churn', ascending=False), 
                 annot=True, fmt='.0%', cmap='YlOrBr', cbar=False);
plt.title('Features Influencing Churn (Pearson)', fontsize=17);

plt.subplot(1, 2, 2)
sns.heatmap(pd.DataFrame(phik['churn']).drop(index='churn').sort_values('churn', ascending=False), 
                 annot=True, fmt='.0%', cmap='YlOrBr', cbar=False);
plt.title('Features Influencing Churn (Phik)', fontsize=17);

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Features influencing churn: Pearson vs Phik correlation

title: Churn Analysis for Bank Customers
text:
Key Findings






TipMain Insights




Credit score is the strongest churn driver — as scores increase, so does the churn rate
Number of products reveals a clear pattern: customers with 1 product have only 10% churn, those with 2 products almost never leave, but customers with 3-4 products churn almost entirely
Age shows increasing churn from age 20 to 40, a small spike at 55, and almost no churn after 60
Recent activity correlates with churn — likely because customers transfer funds before closing accounts
Income and balance — customers with fewer funds are less likely to churn, while those earning above 45,000 show higher churn rates

title: Churn Analysis for Bank Customers
text:
Results
The analysis revealed several actionable patterns for the bank’s marketing department.
The bank successfully attracts credit and salary-transfer customers, but struggles to retain clients with other products. Customers with 3-4 products are almost entirely churned, suggesting that beyond credit services, the bank’s product offerings may not meet customer expectations.

The data suggests a critical gap: the bank lacks a compelling product for higher-income customers — the “upper middle class” segment earning above the regional average salary.

The relationship between recent activity and churn is particularly interesting. Customers who were recently active are more likely to leave, which makes sense — before closing an account, customers typically transfer their funds to other banks. This activity spike could serve as an early warning signal for the retention team.






WarningRecommendation



The bank should focus on developing premium products for customers with higher incomes (above 45,000) and consider personalised retention strategies for customers aged 30-45, who represent the highest churn risk segment.







Next: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection →

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Disinformation has become a defining challenge of the digital age. This project compares two fundamentally different AI approaches to fake news detection: BERT (a discriminative model, fine-tuned on labelled data) and LLaMA 3 (a generative model, using few-shot prompting via Ollama). The goal was to determine which approach is more suitable for deployment in a news organisation’s editorial pipeline.
 View BERT notebook on GitHub  View LLaMA 3 notebook on GitHub

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Disinformation has become a defining challenge of the digital age. This project compares two fundamentally different AI approaches to fake news detection: BERT (a discriminative model, fine-tuned on labelled data) and LLaMA 3 (a generative model, using few-shot prompting via Ollama). The goal was to determine which approach is more suitable for deployment in a news organisation’s editorial pipeline.
 View BERT notebook on GitHub  View LLaMA 3 notebook on GitHub

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Dataset
The ISOT Fake and Real News Dataset contains 44,919 labelled news articles collected from Reuters (real) and Politifact-flagged sources (fake).








Feature
Real News
Fake News




Articles
21,417
23,502


Source
Reuters.com
Politifact/Wikipedia-flagged


Topics
politicsNews, worldnews
politics, Government News, US_News


Date range
2016–2018
2015–2018

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Data Preparation


Code
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report, confusion_matrix, ConfusionMatrixDisplay
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')

# Simulating the dataset summary (original data loaded from CSVs in full notebook)
data = {
    'Category': ['Real', 'Fake'],
    'Count': [21417, 23502],
    'Avg Words': [405, 688]
}
summary_df = pd.DataFrame(data)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 4))

axes[0].bar(summary_df['Category'], summary_df['Count'],
            color=['steelblue', 'tomato'], edgecolor='white')
axes[0].set_title('Class Distribution (Full Dataset)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Number of Articles')
for i, v in enumerate(summary_df['Count']):
    axes[0].text(i, v + 300, f'{v:,}', ha='center')

axes[1].bar(summary_df['Category'], summary_df['Avg Words'],
            color=['steelblue', 'tomato'], edgecolor='white')
axes[1].set_title('Average Word Count per Article')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Words')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Class distribution and word count analysis




A balanced subsample of 4,000 articles (2,000 real + 2,000 fake) was used for BERT fine-tuning, split 80/20 into training and test sets. For LLaMA 3, a separate balanced sample of 50 articles was drawn from the held-out test set due to computational constraints of running the model locally on CPU.

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
BERT Fine-Tuning Approach
The pre-trained bert-base-uncased model was fine-tuned using the HuggingFace Transformers library with the following configuration:



Hyperparameter
Value




Learning rate
2e-5


Batch size (train/eval)
16 / 32


Epochs
3


Max sequence length
256 tokens


Warmup steps
200


Metric for best model
F1 (macro)





Code
# Simulated training curves based on actual results
steps = list(range(0, 600, 50))
train_loss = [0.65, 0.25, 0.08, 0.03, 0.015, 0.01, 0.008, 0.005, 0.004, 0.003, 0.002, 0.002]
epochs = [1, 2, 3]
val_loss = [0.029, 0.017, 0.019]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 4))
axes[0].plot(steps, train_loss, color='steelblue', linewidth=2)
axes[0].set_title('Training Loss')
axes[0].set_xlabel('Steps')
axes[0].set_ylabel('Loss')

axes[1].plot(epochs, val_loss, marker='o', color='tomato', linewidth=2)
axes[1].set_title('Validation Loss per Epoch')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Epoch')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Loss')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





BERT training and validation loss curves

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
LLaMA 3 Few-Shot Approach
LLaMA 3 was run locally using Ollama — an open-source runtime for large language models. No model weights were updated; instead, a few-shot prompt with three labelled examples was used to guide classification.


Code
# Few-shot prompt template used for LLaMA 3
prompt_template = """
You are an expert fact-checker. Classify each news article as REAL or FAKE.
Only reply with one word: REAL or FAKE.

Examples:
Article: "WASHINGTON (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate confirmed Rex Tillerson 
as secretary of state by a vote of 56-43."
Label: REAL

Article: "BREAKING: Anonymous sources confirm the deep state is planning 
to overthrow the president using secret FEMA camps."
Label: FAKE

Article: "Scientists ADMIT vaccines cause autism. The mainstream media 
is hiding the TRUTH from you!"
Label: FAKE

Now classify this article:
Article: "{text}"
Label:
"""
print(prompt_template)



You are an expert fact-checker. Classify each news article as REAL or FAKE.
Only reply with one word: REAL or FAKE.

Examples:
Article: "WASHINGTON (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate confirmed Rex Tillerson 
as secretary of state by a vote of 56-43."
Label: REAL

Article: "BREAKING: Anonymous sources confirm the deep state is planning 
to overthrow the president using secret FEMA camps."
Label: FAKE

Article: "Scientists ADMIT vaccines cause autism. The mainstream media 
is hiding the TRUTH from you!"
Label: FAKE

Now classify this article:
Article: "{text}"
Label:

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Results Comparison


Code
import plotly.graph_objects as go

metrics = ['Accuracy', 'Macro Precision', 'Macro Recall', 'Macro F1']
bert_scores = [99.75, 99.75, 99.75, 99.75]
llama_scores = [54.00, 76.04, 54.00, 41.65]

fig = go.Figure()
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='BERT (Fine-tuned)', x=metrics, y=bert_scores,
                     marker_color='steelblue'))
fig.add_trace(go.Bar(name='LLaMA 3 (Few-shot)', x=metrics, y=llama_scores,
                     marker_color='tomato'))

fig.update_layout(
    title='Model Performance Comparison (%)',
    yaxis_title='Score (%)',
    barmode='group',
    plot_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    paper_bgcolor='rgba(0,0,0,0)',
    yaxis=dict(range=[0, 105])
)
fig.show()


                                                
Head-to-head performance comparison: BERT vs LLaMA 3




Code
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8, 4))

# BERT confusion matrix
bert_cm = np.array([[400, 0], [2, 398]])
disp1 = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=bert_cm, display_labels=['Fake', 'Real'])
disp1.plot(ax=axes[0], colorbar=False, cmap='Blues')
axes[0].set_title('BERT (800 articles)')

# LLaMA confusion matrix
llama_cm = np.array([[2, 23], [0, 25]])
disp2 = ConfusionMatrixDisplay(confusion_matrix=llama_cm, display_labels=['Fake', 'Real'])
disp2.plot(ax=axes[1], colorbar=False, cmap='Oranges')
axes[1].set_title('LLaMA 3 (50 articles)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Confusion matrices for both models

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Key Findings






TipMain Insights




BERT achieved 99.75% accuracy with perfect recall on fake articles — only 2 out of 800 articles were misclassified
LLaMA 3 achieved just 54% accuracy (barely above random chance) with a strong bias towards predicting “Real” — 23 out of 25 fake articles were missed
The performance gap is not a failure of LLaMA 3 as a model, but a limitation of few-shot prompting versus gradient-based fine-tuning for structured classification tasks









WarningBusiness Recommendation



For deployment in a news organisation’s editorial pipeline, BERT is the clear choice — its 99.75% accuracy translates to roughly 25 misclassifications per 10,000 articles. However, a hybrid architecture combining BERT’s classification reliability with LLaMA 3’s generative explainability could offer the best of both worlds: BERT flags articles, LLaMA 3 explains why.

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Ethical Considerations
This project evaluated both models against the EU AI Act (2024) and Trustworthy AI principles. Key considerations include maintaining human oversight in editorial decisions, addressing dataset bias (the ISOT dataset reflects Western media perspectives), and ensuring transparency in model decision-making. LLaMA 3’s local deployment via Ollama offers a privacy advantage, as no article text leaves the organisation’s infrastructure.

title: BERT vs LLaMA 3: Fake News Detection
text:
Methodology





flowchart LR
    A[ISOT Dataset<br>44,919 articles] --> B[Preprocessing<br>Label assignment<br>Feature construction]
    B --> C[BERT Pipeline<br>4,000 articles<br>Fine-tuning]
    B --> D[LLaMA 3 Pipeline<br>50 articles<br>Few-shot prompting]
    C --> E[Evaluation<br>Accuracy, F1<br>Confusion Matrix]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Comparative<br>Analysis]







This project was completed as part of the MSc in Business Analytics, Advanced Analytics Techniques module at Dublin City University.
Due to computational restraints, visualisations on this page are recreated manually.

title: Reflection
text: Thanks to this module I deepened my expertise in coding, which is very valuable for a finance professional who is seeking to transition into data analytics. Now I am equipped with enough knowledge to build a website to showcase my projects build during a study in DCU and also outside of it.

title: Reflection
text: Thanks to this module I deepened my expertise in coding, which is very valuable for a finance professional who is seeking to transition into data analytics. Now I am equipped with enough knowledge to build a website to showcase my projects build during a study in DCU and also outside of it.

title: Reflection
text:
Technical Skills Acquired

Git & Version Control
Before this module I used Git just once only for uploading my Python notebooks. Learning to commit, push, and manage repositories was initially challenging, but thanks to very detailed and understandable instructions it become very easy to process.


Quarto & SCSS
I already had experience with Web Design but more from a vusual perspective. Learning YAML, SCSS and Markdown helped me get a different perspective on it. Styling websites with SCSS proved to be one of the most enjoyable aspects of the project.


Markdown & Rich Content
Learning all the different ways to customise the content through Markdown available in Quarto such as making callout blocks, including diagrams and tables of content, adding code folding, etc. showed me how much can be achieved without touching raw HTML.


VS Code & Workflow
Using VS Code to edit files and make commits, run commands, check out the site, as well as use Git brought everything together into one efficient workflow.

title: Reflection
text:
Challenges & How I Overcame Them

“Every error message is a lesson in disguise.”

One of the biggest challenges was understanding the repositories and folders relationships. Early on, I accidentally pushed files to the wrong repository and had to learn how to fix its structures. To overcome this I needed to learn new commands and be more careful from the beginning of the project.
Another challenge was understanding navigation between projects. I didn’t like the automatic navigation via sidebar, so I had to learn how to create custom navigation links in the content of each project page.

title: Reflection
text:
Connecting to My Professional Goals
As a finance professional building a data science portfolio, the skills I acquired are deeply connected with my goals:

Professional presence — having a polished portfolio website to showcase my work
Technical communication — presenting projects with interactive visualisations, clean layouts, and clear writing
Version control — a foundational skill for any collaborative or technical role
Web literacy — understanding how sites are built, styled, and deployed







TipLooking Forward



I plan to continue developing my data science skills, focusing on machine learning applications in finance, and contributing to open-source projects. This website will serve as a living portfolio that grows with my career.

title: Reflection
text:
What I Would Do Differently
If I were to start this module again, I would:

Set up my Git repository structure correctly from the very beginning
Open only the project folder in VS Code — never a parent directory
Spend more time planning the website design before writing any SCSS
Commit more frequently with descriptive messages from day one

title: Reflection
text:
Final Thoughts
This module gave me the confidence to build something from scratch and put it on the internet. The combination of technical tools — Quarto, Git, SCSS, Markdown — and the design thinking required to make it all cohesive was a uniquely satisfying learning experience.

title: Fraud Detection Using Statistics and Machine Learning
text: Financial fraud is a growing concern for institutions worldwide. This project analyses 500 customer transaction records to identify fraud patterns and build predictive models. The dataset reveals that 49% of transactions are fraudulent, amounting to €355K in losses — 35.5% of total transaction volume.

title: Fraud Detection Using Statistics and Machine Learning
text: Financial fraud is a growing concern for institutions worldwide. This project analyses 500 customer transaction records to identify fraud patterns and build predictive models. The dataset reveals that 49% of transactions are fraudulent, amounting to €355K in losses — 35.5% of total transaction volume.

title: Fraud Detection Using Statistics and Machine Learning
text:
The Critical Threshold
One of the most striking findings was the relationship between transaction size and fraud probability.


Code
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore')
sns.set_style('whitegrid')

df = pd.read_excel("fraud_victim_dataset.xlsx")
df_new = pd.get_dummies(df,
                    columns=['gender', 'education', 'marital_status',
                             'region', 'transaction_type', 'device_type'],
                    drop_first=True)

df_new['amount_bin'] = (df_new['transaction_amount'] // 1000) * 1000
fraud_rate = df_new.groupby('amount_bin')['is_fraud'].mean() * 100

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
ax = sns.barplot(x=fraud_rate.index.astype(int), y=fraud_rate.values, palette='flare')
ax.set_xticklabels([f"{int(x/1000)}K" for x in fraud_rate.index])
for container in ax.containers:
    ax.bar_label(container, fmt='%.0f%%')
plt.title('Fraud Rate per Transaction Amount Bin', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('Transaction Amount (EUR)')
plt.ylabel('Fraud Rate (%)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Fraud rate increases sharply above €6,000










ImportantCritical Finding



Fraud rates rise sharply from €8K, reaching 80–100% for transactions above €9K. Transactions above €6K show a clear linear relationship between transaction size and fraud loss.

title: Fraud Detection Using Statistics and Machine Learning
text:
Transaction vs Fraud Amount


Code
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='transaction_amount', y='fraud_amount',
                hue='is_fraud', palette='Set2', alpha=0.7)
plt.title('Transaction Amount vs Fraud Amount', fontsize=14)
plt.xlabel('Transaction Amount (EUR)')
plt.ylabel('Fraud Amount (EUR)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Clear separation between fraud and non-fraud transactions

title: Fraud Detection Using Statistics and Machine Learning
text:
Model Comparison
Four classification models were tested. Random Forest achieved the best overall performance. ## Model Comparison
Four classification models were tested for fraud detection:










Metric
Logistic Regression
Random Forest
Gradient Boosting
XGBoost




Accuracy
0.60
0.66
0.62
0.58


Precision
0.58
0.64
0.59
0.56


Recall
0.69
0.70
0.69
0.72


F1-score
0.63
0.67
0.64
0.63


ROC-AUC
0.66
0.70
0.63
0.62



Random Forest achieved the best overall performance with ROC-AUC of 0.704, confirming that fraud patterns in this dataset are non-linear. XGBoost showed the highest recall (72%) but lacked precision, leading to more false positives.

title: Fraud Detection Using Statistics and Machine Learning
text:
Key Findings






TipMain Insights




Transaction amount is the strongest fraud predictor — rates exceed 80% above €8K
Age matters — fraud cases grow significantly after age 60
Lower-income customers face higher fraud rates
Desktop and mobile channels show higher fraud rates than other devices
Random Forest outperformed all models with ROC-AUC of 0.704

title: Fraud Detection Using Statistics and Machine Learning
text:
Methodology





flowchart TD
    A[Customer Dataset<br>500 records, 23 features] --> B[Data Cleaning<br>Outlier detection via IQR & Z-score]
    B --> C[EDA<br>Distribution & correlation analysis]
    C --> D[Classification<br>4 models compared]
    C --> E[Regression<br>Fraud amount prediction]
    D --> F[Recommendations]
    E --> F












WarningBusiness Recommendations




Additional verification for transactions above €6K
Targeted protection for low-income and elderly customers
Enhanced security on desktop and mobile channels
Deploy Random Forest as the primary fraud detection engine




This project was completed as part of the MSc in Business Analytics programme at Dublin City University.
